title: 'Maestro': Holy Sh*t. Plus, the Top 10 Performances of 2023.
format: Sean and Amanda count down their favorite performances of 2023, the first of three year-end top 10s, steering clear of the locks they'll be arguing about at the Oscars. Then a long, divided sit with Bradley Cooper's Maestro: gorgeous, moving in stretches, and hard to pin down on what it's actually about.

segments.heading: The Top 10 Performances of 2023
segments.note: Sean and Amanda, counting down, ties and cheats included

picks.title: Anatomy of a Fall
picks.note: No. 1. Sandra Hüller, one film of two: the capital-A acting turn, all speeches and crying, as a writer accused of killing her husband.

picks.title: The Zone of Interest
picks.note: No. 1, the other Hüller. As the Auschwitz commandant's wife, a performance so unshowy it feels observed rather than acted. Two opposite modes in one year is Sean's acting achievement of the year.

picks.title: The Iron Claw
picks.note: No. 2. Zac Efron as Kevin Von Erich: the livewire weirdness that flopped in Baywatch turned magnetic, body and all.

picks.title: Infinity Pool
picks.note: No. 3, scream queens (tie). Mia Goth with the restrictor plate off in Brandon Cronenberg's chaos, the funniest performance of the year to Sean.

picks.title: Talk to Me
picks.note: No. 3, scream queens (tie). Sophie Wilde carrying the summer's horror breakout through a cliché the movie leans on and she outruns.

picks.title: Passages
picks.note: No. 4. Franz Rogowski as Ira Sachs's charismatic disaster: the toxic almost-boyfriend you can't cut out. Sean's breakout of the year for him in English.

picks.title: Saltburn
picks.note: No. 5. Rosamund Pike as the only person in the movie who seems to know what it's about, a character part done exactly right.

picks.title: All of Us Strangers
picks.note: No. 1 (a cheat, three ways): Andrew Scott, Claire Foy, and Jamie Bell as an unusual family. Wept openly, Amanda's first-saddest movie of the year, ahead of Wonka.

picks.title: Renaissance: A Film by Beyoncé
picks.note: No. 2. Beyoncé, whom Amanda calls the performer of a generation, in a movie that got ignored because concert films do.

picks.title: Are You There God? It's Me, Margaret.
picks.note: No. 3. Rachel McAdams, shout-out to moms. On a lot of critics' lists, a longshot at the Academy.

picks.title: American Fiction
picks.note: No. 4. Sterling K. Brown with the trickiest part in the movie, holding the family drama together. Cord Jefferson's adaptation, more on it later this week.

picks.title: BlackBerry
picks.note: No. 5, supporting work (tie): Glenn Howerton as the maniacal striver who runs the company, the most yelling in a performance all year.

picks.title: Oppenheimer
picks.note: No. 5, supporting work (tie): David Krumholtz as the film's agent of empathy, boxed out of a campaign by Downey and Damon.

picks.title: Earth Mama
picks.note: Breakout. Tia Nomore in what is remarkably her first screen performance.

picks.title: The Holdovers
picks.note: Breakout. Dominic Sessa, never on screen before, holding his own with Da'Vine Joy Randolph and Paul Giamatti.

picks.title: M3GAN
picks.note: Breakout. Amy Donald as the physical performer behind Megan, Amanda's Andy Serkis of 2023.

picks.title: Priscilla
picks.note: Honorable mention. Cailee Spaeny, kept off the main list only because Priscilla was in Sean's top five.

picks.title: Mission: Impossible - Dead Reckoning Part One
picks.note: Honorable mention. Hayley Atwell standing toe to toe with Cruise. Sean's fix: cut the first 15 minutes and it's a five-star movie.

picks.title: Memory
picks.note: Honorable mention (not out yet). Jessica Chastain, one of the best actors alive, doing it in the small movie no one will see.

picks.title: Past Lives
picks.note: Honorable mention. Teo Yoo and John Magaro, the two men whose odd pairing makes the triangle work.

picks.title: May December
picks.note: Honorable mention. Charles Melton, the last 30 minutes the movie hands to him as it becomes his crisis.

segments.heading: 'Maestro'
segments.note: Bradley Cooper's Leonard Bernstein biopic, now on Netflix

films.title: Maestro
films.note: Cooper's second film as director, co-written with Josh Singer, shot by Matthew Libatique, with Carey Mulligan as Felicia Montealegre. Scene by scene it's gorgeous, the Ely Cathedral Mahler 2 the highlight, but both hosts come away puzzled about what it's actually about: it wants to be the story of the marriage and can't stop being the story of the man. Sean's read: Cooper, like Bernstein, is a "high bro" at war with his own ambition, and the movie is too self-aware for its own good.

referenced.group: Bernstein on screen, and Cooper's touchstones
